summary: Assign explicit ID to Export As menu

description.text:
Export As... menu option in scene has no explicit ID, causing the option to take whatever comes after the last option, and duplicating it (as another option was using that implicit ID explicitly). If anything is added before that option, it will be shifted into a different ID and malfunction (as seen in #112894).

This PR assigns an explicit ID. While it does fix the said issue, it does not replace the other PR. The newly added option had actually inconsistent name/position.

summary: C# DLL not updated on rebuild after system time manipulation

description.text:
### Tested versions

### System information

Windows10 - Godot-v4.5.1.stable.mono.official [f62fdbde1]

### Issue description

I've encountered a potential bug in the Godot C# version where modifying the system time can prevent subsequent C# code changes from being reflected in the built DLL.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Have a Godot C# project with some existing C# scripts.
2. Change the system time on your local machine forward (e.g., from 2025-11-18 to 2025-11-20).
3. Make a trivial change to a C# script (e.g., add a comment, modify a variable) and build the project within Godot. Observe that the build succeeds.
4. Change the system time back to its original/correct time.
5. Make another distinct change to the same or a different C# script, for example, print "hello world" in '_Ready'. Build the project again.
6. Start the game in editor, no print showing in output panel.
7. Move to `.godot\mono\temp\bin\Debug` ,  the dll's last update time is 2025-11-20, not 2025-11-18

summary: Fix cannot remove scene root when reload scene

description.text:
- *Fixes: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112880*
- *Follow up: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/112401*

## Issue Description

When we move a instanced scene file cross different folders in `FileSystemDock`, we will call `reload_scene()` to reload both `main.tscn` and `sub.tscn`.

Currently, we call `memdelete()` to delete root node of scene immediately. It will be considered as an accidental deletion.

Because `remove_scene()` not only delete root node, but also delete whole scene, so it won't crash before.

## Solution

We can change `memdelete()` to `queue_free()`, to free root node defer. In the current frame, we will delete relevant scene, then next frame won't consider root node cannot be deleted.

## MRP

[test-112880.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23596729/test-112880.zip)

[remove-root-node.webm](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/f208af1c-1407-42de-9ad2-7d7ad6093dc1)

summary: Dialog UI element hierarchy incorrect — children not under dialog role

description.text:
### Additional metadata (for maintainers)
- Suggested labels: accessibility, GUI, dialog, bug
- Type: bug
- Severity: important for screen-reader users

### Tested versions

**Godot version**: 4.6.dev4 and 4.5.1-Stable (oficial build)  

### System information

Godot v4.5.1.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - OpenGL ES 3/ANGLE (Compatibility) - ANGLE (Intel, Intel(R) HD Graphics 4600 (0x00000412) Direct3D11 vs_5_0 ps_5_0, D3D11-20.19.15.4531) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-4570S CPU @ 2.90GHz (4 threads) - 11.91 GiB memory - NVDA screen reader  

### Issue description

When a dialog (AcceptDialog / ConfirmationDialog) appears, its UI elements (buttons, labels) are exposed as children of the main window rather than as children of the dialog container. The dialog container itself lacks an accessible node with role="dialog".  
This means:
- the dialog is not recognized as a dialog by screen readers  
- controls are not semantically grouped under the dialog  
- the accessible tree is incorrect (children under root window)  
- no specific “dialog” role and hierarchy


### Steps to reproduce

1. Open a project in Godot 4.6.dev4 or Previous versions
2. Trigger a dialog (e.g., AcceptDialog)  
3. Observe with NVDA that the dialog announcement is missing and controls appear as part of the main window hierarchy  
4. Inspect accessible tree (AccessKit) and note children appear under root_window instead of dialog element


### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

**Expected behavior**:  
- A node with role="dialog" is created for the dialog container  
- All dialog internal controls appear as children of that dialog element  
- Screen reader announces “Dialog: <title>” and lists its contents  
- Focus remains logically within the dialog container while it is open

**Final note**:
Thank you in advance for taking the time to look into this issue. I am a real daily screen-reader user, and AccessKit has finally made it possible for me to use Godot independently to build the games I dream of creating. This level of accessibility is not theoretical for me — it directly affects my ability to work as a blind developer.
This is my first issue ever submitted to the Godot project, and I’m committed to reporting every accessibility problem I encounter. Accessibility is my area of expertise, and I’m fully willing to collaborate so the engine can continue growing and become even more inclusive.
Small improvements like this have a huge impact on real users, and I sincerely appreciate any attention this report may receive. I hope it can be considered for an upcoming development build.

summary: Fix excessive draw calls when viewing 3D collision shape gizmos

description.text:
## Description
This PR fixes the performance regression introduced in v4.4 where enabling gizmos in the 3D editor causes excessive draw calls and poor performance.

## Changes
- Changed Shape3D.debug_fill default from true to false in scene/resources/3d/shape_3d.h
- Updated documentation in CollisionShape3D.xml and CollisionPolygon3D.xml to reflect the new default

## Technical Details
When debug_fill is enabled, collision shapes render both a wireframe and a solid fill. This causes exponential increases in draw calls for scenes with many collision shapes. The issue was bisected by the community to commit 0fc082e (PR #90644).

Testing shows minimal visual difference between filled and wireframe-only gizmos, while performance improves dramatically. Users can still opt-in to filled gizmos by setting the property to true if desired.

## Testing
- Verified syntax of all changes
- Changes are minimal (3 files, 3 lines changed)
- Community testing in issue thread confirms performance improvement

Fixes #103676

summary: GLTF: Support MultiMesh objects using `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing`

description.text: Fixes issue #109280, supersedes PR #107866. Draft because this depends on PR #113202, PR #113203, PR #113206, PR #113245, and PR #113247. This PR adds support for importing glTF `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing` nodes as Godot MultiMesh nodes: In master, this extension was not supported, which meant that such objects were not imported as multiple instances. This PR also adds support for exporting MultiMesh nodes as glTF `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing`, and configuring the export behavior. Users can choose to use the extension, use plain glTF nodes, or use both: Note that the dropdown only appears when the scene contains at least one MultiMeshInstance3D node, so it won't clutter the export settings UI in most cases where the user does not have any such nodes in their scene. The options have the following behavior: * Optional EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing: Convert MultiMesh nodes into the `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing` extension. The extension is marked as not required, so implementations without support can still load the file, but they will see a single mesh instead of multiple instances of meshes. * Required EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing: Convert MultiMesh nodes into the `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing` extension. The extension is marked as required, so implementations without support cannot load the file. * Multiple Nodes Only: Convert MultiMesh nodes into multiple glTF nodes. This is similar to the existing behavior in master, but there are multiple bugs with the current master, which have been fixed in this PR. * Multiple Nodes Fallback: Convert MultiMesh nodes into both the `EXT_mesh_gpu_instancing` extension and multiple nodes, allowing it to be imported as a MultiMesh when supported, or imported as nodes when not supported. Here's what it looks like when loading a glTF exported with "Multiple Nodes Fallback" into Godot 4.5-stable: This PR also makes several related changes: * Adjust `SceneImportSettingsDialog::_fill_scene` to handle MultiMeshInstance3D nodes. * Add a new function `_export_configure_for_scene` to GLTFDocumentExtension, allowing extensions to be configured before the export process starts, so that they can customize what properties are shown. * Unify Mesh to ImporterMesh conversion logic and move it to `ImporterMesh::from_mesh`. * Add a function to GLTFState to generate a unique name (previously was private to GLTFDocument). * Fix a bug where MultiMesh's own transform was multiplied into each instance. * Fix a bug where 2D MultiMesh transforms were swapping the Y coordinate into Z. * Fix a bug where the material name was used as the surface name instead of the surface name. * Fix an issue where extensions added export settings before the base glTF settings finished being added. * Fix an issue where buffers and accessors were not loaded by the time nodes were parsed. Nodes can depend on buffers and accessors, so the buffers and accessors need to be loaded first. * Fix an issue where buffers and accessors were serialized before nodes were serialized. Exported nodes can write data to buffers and accessors, so the buffers and accessors need to be serialized after.

summary: global_transform.basis.get_scale() is affected by rotation

the get_scale() on the basis class stats that it should not be influenced by rotation but it is.
a work around it to convert the scale to a string rather then vec3. 

https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_basis.html#class-basis-method-get-scale

It might be useful to add a get_scale_str() function in the base class that returns the string value that will filter out minor differences caused by rotation.
 
then update the documentation, so that the limitations of both functions are displayed:

get_scale()
results will be affected by changes in rotation

get_scale_str()
Won't be affected by changes in rotation, but will not pick up minor scale adjustment's. e.g. scale 1.0 to 1.0001 on 1 axis will not trigger a difference

I have highlighted this in another error thread however its been closed as a duplicate as its misinterpreted as an error in the skeletonmodifier3D class however that is not the case, but was what I originally though the error to be as I didn't think get_scale() was affected by rotation given the documentation explicitly ruling that out.

https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112756#issuecomment-3536462979

open the MRP

- rotate the Basic cube
- adjust the scale of STR cube
- adjust the scale of the Basic cube

both are checking for size differences each farm with the get_scale() function 

you will see that the STR behaves as you would expected the get_scale() function to work
the basic throws out the print command every time that should only be done on a scale change even if its only rotated.

summary: Fix crash in `EditorFileDialog` by checking for null pointer

description.text:

To speed up the contribution process and avoid CI errors, please set up pre-commit hooks locally:
https://contributing.godotengine.org/en/latest/engine/guidelines/code_style.html

Fixes bug report #112855, in which opening the following nested windows caused a crash:

summary: CSG interferes with drop raycast by detecting itself

description.text:
### Tested versions

Godot v4.6.dev4

### System information

Windows 11 (build 26200) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated Intel(R) Iris(R) Xe Graphics (Intel Corporation; 32.0.101.7076) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-1135G7 @ 2.40GHz (8 threads) - 15.80 GiB memory

### Issue description

I’m using several prefabricated CSG wall and floor scenes for level blockout. When collision is enabled on these CSG nodes, it becomes almost impossible to place them precisely in the editor using drag-and-drop

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b91f4623-cffb-4170-841e-e18833428f41

After some debugging, I found that the drag placement raycast hits the CSG node being dragged, causing the preview to snap to its own surface instead of the environment.

The relevant line is here:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/ef34c3d534a16b140aef0643ed2fa36f8e9d1612/editor/scene/3d/node_3d_editor_plugin.cpp#L4873

`StaticBody3D` is ignored by `intersect_ray`, but CSG nodes are not, so they block the ray

### Steps to reproduce

- Create a new scene
- Add a `CSGBox3D` and enable collisions
- Save the scene
- Drag and drop this scene into another scene in the editor
- Attempt to position it using the mouse - the object snaps to its own collision instead of the target surface

summary: The modern theme lacks some interaction feedback in comparison to the classic theme

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in 4.6-dev3 ### System information Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS ### Issue description Interaction feedback is important. It conveys immediate confirmation to the users' actions and a sense a speed and quality. I noticed that, due to some color generation choices in the modern theme, some of the interaction feedback in this theme is not perceptible, in comparison to the classic theme. I will begin by a bit of theory before explaining the actual bug, because this point can be more or less sucessfully understood by different audiences - speaking by experience. ## Interaction feedback theory IMHO, good interaction feedback should happen in all different steps of a user interaction. Since Godot is mostly interacted with a mouse - although the following is valid using a finger on a touchscreen as well - let's take example of basic interactions. Here is the main point : **A mouse click is not a single discrete event** - it is compound of two : - the button being **pressed**, or down, - and the button being **released**, or up. Those never happen at the exact same time, even if just milliseconds apart. The two events deserve separate feedback. Feedback means to show a confirmation on screen at the exact moment the event occurs by occuring an immediate change in graphics or colors, so that the user can directly make an "action = reaction" haptic association. An interaction event can be the part of a longer chain of events that takes time. Showing timely interaction is crucial even if the event is not fully done yet. For example, if something should change after loading when ticking a checkbox, I find it to be frustrating to feel the checkbox change only after the loading is done. Immediate feedback is basically telling the user "yes, I heard you", even if the task behind requires more time. The way many web interfaces are implemented give a "bad" example of timely interaction feedback. The pressed state is often forgotten, the wait for server-side confirmation before state change can make the things feel slow, and the use of slow fade transitions create an impression of sluggishness. ### Button The button click has three states on normal interaction. **Normal, Hover, Pressed.** **Interaction example for a button** Normal : the button in its idle state Hover : the mouse approaches the button and hovers it Pressed : the user presses and holds the mouse button down Hover (again) : the user releases the mouse button -> **the main button event fires at this moment** Normal : the mouse moves away from the button Eventually, the focus can add up to those, triggered by the button down stage or keyboard focus. ### Two-state element (toggle button, checkbox, menu, dropdown, foldable pane, tabs...) The basic interaction states are still the same: **Normal, Hover, Pressed.** We mix it up with the two states of the togglable element: **Unchecked, Checked, (Transient / Undecided / "third state")** _The third state is very rare but worth mentioning - can be for example reflecting a group selection state of both checked and unchecked items thus not able to determine. We will ignore it for now._ The mix gives those combinations: **Normal, Hover, Pressed, Checked, Hover+Checked, Pressed+Checked.** What does correspond to the checked state? - Checkbox, radio button, toggle button : checked (obviously) - Tab : when this is the currently active tab - Foldable pane : when the pane is unfolded - Dropdown : when the dropdown menu or selector is visible - Menu headers, like the File / Edit... menus of desktop apps : when the corresponding menu is open - **Interaction example for a checkbox** Normal : the checkbox is unchecked Hover : the mouse approaches the button and hovers it Pressed : the user presses and holds the mouse button down Hover + checked : the user releases the mouse button -> **the checkbox becomes checked at this point** Checked : the mouse moves away from the button Hover + checked : the mouse moves back onto the checked checkbox Pressed + checked : the user presses and holds the mouse button down Hover : the user releases the mouse button -> **the checkbox becomes unchecked at this point** Normal : the mouse moves away from the checkbox ### Transitions between states In the two examples above, each transition should be visible on the screen. Else it can feel clunky, slow, unresponding and cause frustration. The item that should show the interaction feedback is the one which is touched directly. For example, not showing a button hover change while assuming that a on-hover tooltip would do the job still feels strange. What can be realistically done is also dependent on the current limitations of the engine. For example, the current implementation of the button in its toggle mode (not the checkbox) locks it in the pressed state when it is checked instead of handling a distinct checked aspect. ## Spots with missing interaction feedback ### Checkboxes The difference between states is barely perceptible. Here, there is no perceptible feedback while pressing the button down on an unchecked checkbox. Same thing when hovering a checked checkbox. This is frustrating. The classic theme shows a better differenciation of hover and pressed state changes. ### Menus No difference between the hover and "menu open" states. The menu header item does not appear to respond itself to the "button down" interaction. Showing hover / "menu open" Mouse down gives no reaction = frustrating. The classic theme shows a difference. ### Bottom buttons No difference between the hover and "palette open" states i.e. button toggled or checked. Showing hover / checked. Mouse down gives no reaction = frustrating. The classic theme shows a difference. ### Tree items The hover color is the same as the selected one. We should be able to tell the difference, with a different opacity for example. Classic theme ### Foldable sections in inspector No feedback on hover. Showing normal / hover. The classic theme shows a hover feedback. ### Dropdown lists in inspector Missing feedback when open. Showing normal / hover / open. Classic theme This is due to the nature of the "flat" buttons that only rely on the text color. The focus rectangle has been removed on click as well and served as some sort of click feedback until then. ## Takeaways This can be fixed by some fine-tuning in the points I mentioned. Making proper interaction feedback goes a long way in the quality feeling of the editor! ### Steps to reproduce Create an empty project. Play around with the elements I mentioned. Swap between the Classic and Modern themes to feel the difference (I insist on "feel"). ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) N/A

summary: Weird visual artifacts in right eye with foveated rendering enabled in Vulkan Mobile renderer on Meta Quest 3

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in `master` after 3c1e4792909fbaff63e2437f1c06029bd1aa507f - NOT reproducible in Godot 4.5.1 ### System information Meta Quest 3 - Android - Qualcomm XR2 gen2 Also reproducible on Samsung Galaxy XR with Qualcomm XR2+ gen2 (<-- the extra plus) ### Issue description With foveated rendering enabled in the Vulkan Mobile renderer, there is a sort of "insect eye" or "kaleidoscope" thing happening in the right eye: The above image was captured in RenderDoc. In the background the the view looks more-or-less correct, but then there are 6 smaller views of different subsets of the scene shown along the left and right edges. This is a regression caused by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/110077 @clayjohn spent some time looking into it, and it appears to be a driver bug that is triggered by the use of spec constants in this particular case. With some modifications, he found that https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/111452 would fix it (I think because it optimizes away the spec constants) ### Steps to reproduce With an XR project using the Vulkan Mobile renderer, you only need to ensure that foveated rendering is enabled by: - Setting "XR" -> "OpenXR" -> "Foveation Level" to "High" (or any other value aside from "Off") - Setting "Rendering" -> "VRS" -> "Mode" to "XR" ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) This isn't exactly an MRP (because it isn't minimal), but the issue is apparent if you take this project and switch the renderer to "Mobile" (it's set to "Compatibility" in the repo): https://github.com/dsnopek/gdquest-tps-demo/tree/standalone-vr-benchmark (Note: the branch is `standalone-vr-benchmark`)

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.5.1, 4.4.1 This might be a regression, because I don't recall having this issue in earlier versions, but maybe I just didn't zoom out far enough. Tested 4.5.1 and 4.4.1 so far. ### System information Godot v4.5.1.stable - Ubuntu 25.10 25.10 on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3050 Ti Laptop GPU - AMD Ryzen 5 5600H with Radeon Graphics (12 threads) - 13.50 GiB memory ### Issue description When I switch my in-editor view of a 3D scene to "Top" (with orthogonal projection) and then press "Switch perspective/orthogonal view" to get back, sometimes the FOV becomes really warped. I think this is related to zoom level. If I then go to "View - Settings", the editor claims that the FOV is still the standard 70; when I press "OK" in that window, FOV fixes itself. ### Steps to reproduce 1. The amount of warp also seems dependent on the distance. 4. Press Kp5 or choose "Switch Perspective/Orthogonal view" in the "Perspective" menu. Now you should see the increased FOV as depicted below. If you're unable to reproduce the bug, try steps 2-3 again but zoom out even further. 5. If you now press "View - Settings", you'll see that the system thinks that this is FOV 70. summary: [3.x] `BlobShadows` - Ensure blob shadow is hidden when light exits tree

description.text:
Fixes bug reported on discord:
When you delete a light with a blob shadow in the editor, the blob shadow remains even after the light is "deleted".
The reason is that light is not truly deleted, it is kept in the undo-redo queue, so the blob shadow never gets deleted (until the scene is closed).

## Solution
The obvious solution is to either delete or hide the blob shadow on exiting the tree, and indeed this is in retrospect better than relying on the light lifetime, as users can detach lights without deleting them.

Here I've chosen the simple solution, to always hide the blob shadow on tree exit. It should be more performant for cases of detaching then reattaching lights, and it makes handling the blob shadow lifetime easier (as it is tied to the light).

## Notes
* I'm not quite sure offhand how the light itself gets hidden on exiting the tree, but no matter, the method in this PR should work well.
* This is quite a nasty bug for real world cases so worth solving. Currently, in games, users would have to ensure lights they are removing from the tree are also deleted.
* I hadn't seen this during development probably because of lack of environmental lighting in test cases, and it doesn't crop up much outside the editor.

summary: LightmapGI produces different and inconsistently aligned lightmaps between primitives and imported meshes

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.0.stable onwards ### System information Godot v4.5.1.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) ### Issue description I'm unclear whether this is intended behaviour as its existed in some form since 4.0, but the lightmap LightmapGI generates for a 1x1m PlaneMesh is not the same as the lightmap it generates for an imported 1x1m plane made in Blender and they are aligned on the meshes differently. This issue is related and possibly intertwined with another issue I just opened: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112768. It strikes me that either there will probably need to be two different fixes for 112768 for primitives and imported meshes or this issue needs to be resolved at the same time, so the generated lightmaps and their alignment across the meshes is the same. The following is a comparison in 4.5.1. As with the other issue, Denoiser and Use Bicubic Filter are both off to get as 'raw' a result as possible and I'm reimporting the .exr as Lossless in Compatibility so you can see the issue more clearly but the problem is unchanged across renderers. Texel size is the default 0.2 for both. So firstly the issue of the lightmaps not being symmetrical when they should be (see https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112768 concerning PlaneMesh) is also true for the imported mesh. The imported mesh lightmap actually looks like it would be symmetrical were it not misaligned, in this case the expected centre has been shifted down and right (+X, +Z) and the texels no longer align with the edges of the mesh. However the lightmap for the imported mesh is also completely different. The lightmaps that are being generated for a PlaneMesh and an imported mesh are different but how they are being applied on to the meshes is also different. There's the obvious thing that the texels are aligned differently to the edges but it would appear for the imported mesh the lightmap is also being rotated 90 degrees clockwise (which explains the different direction of shift when applied to mesh) as the shift is in the same direction in the actual lightmap file. It's also worth nothing that the generated Lightmap Size Hint is different - the PlaneMesh has 7x7, whereas the imported mesh has 6x6. Going back to 4.0 it seems lightmaps have always generated differently for primitives compared to imported meshes. It appears the texels have always been aligned with the edges on primitive meshes and not on imported meshes. It is unclear whether the rotation of the lightmap was occurring in this example as the centre of the light is correctly aligned here as https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112768 only became a problem from 4.1.2 onwards. Moving the light to the right to check and the lightmap of the imported mesh is indeed being rotated 90 degrees clockwise in 4.0. This is because the lightmap in the file is 'facing' a different direction (upwards in this case, instead of to the right) and needs to be corrected. So the rotation has always been present. Unless there is a technical reason that I am not aware of I would expect the lightmap to be identical (or at the very least much more similar) across primitives and imported meshes if they have the same texel density. Please note I have so far only tested this with a plane so I don't know for sure whether it applies to other primitives. ### Steps to reproduce Open the MRP. Toggle the visibility of the MeshInstance3D to see the difference in the lightmaps between it and the imported 1mPlane. If you want to see the texels clearly, in Compatibility select test1.exr and reimport it in Compress Mode Lossless. ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) [LightmapGI_plane_tests.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/23555670/LightmapGI_plane_tests.zip)

summary: LightmapGI producing incorrect, non-symmetrical lightmaps

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.1.2.stable onwards (though not identically) - Not reproducible in 4.1.1.stable and earlier ### System information Godot v4.5.1.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) ### Issue description In 4.5.1, if I use LightmapGI to bake the light from an OmniLight3D on to a 1x1m PlaneMesh sitting centrally below it this is the resulting lightmap. Denoiser and Use Bicubic Filter are both off to get as 'raw' a result as possible. If I switch to Compatibility and reimport the .exr as Lossless you can see the issue more clearly. (As an aside I'm not sure why I can only get this to display like this with no filtering in Compatibility). The lightmap appears to be incorrectly shifting the centre of the light up and right (+X, -Z). So I went back in time and this hasn't always been a problem. In 4.1.1 the lightmap looks as expected with the light centred on the plane, brightest section in the middle and everything symmetrical. The issue first arises in 4.1.2 - although the misalignment is actually different here - this time being a shift up and left (-X, -Z). However when we look at the lightmap file we see that they have actually changed, not just shifted. As this happened between 4.1.1 and 4.1.2 I'm guessing this is caused by https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/81872. Finally the change in the direction of the shift happened in 4.4. Again this is due to the actual generation of the lightmap being changed. The shift changed from up and left to up and right as it is now in 4.5.1. How LightmapGI generates a lightmap has obviously been changed over time for good reasons but it seems to me that this has caused a fundamental problem where the lightmap no longer looks as you would logically expect it to. In this case the lightmap is not symmetrical despite the light being centred over the plane. Presumably this becomes less of an issue with higher resolution lightmaps as the shift distance will be much less, however I should note that these examples were all done with the default setting of 0.2m for a texel so I would expect it to work fine at this resolution. There is also another issue in that the lightmaps generated for a PlaneMesh and an identically sized imported mesh are not the same which could certainly cause problems applying a consistent fix. I'd originally also discussed this here but it was overcomplicating things so I will open a separate issue for it. EDIT - I have now created a new issue for this here: https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112786. I haven't added an MRP as this is not project specific and it can replicated in less than a minute but the MRP in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112786 demonstrates the same thing for both a PlaneMesh and an imported mesh. ### Steps to reproduce Using LightmapGI, bake the light from an OmniLight3D on to a 1x1m PlaneMesh sitting below the light. The light should be centred over the plane on X and Z. In my example screenshots the OmniLight3D is 0.75m above the plane on the Y and has Range 1m, Attenuation 2, Specular 0. Denoiser and Use Bicubic Filter are both off. ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) The MRP in https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/112786 demonstrates the same thing for both a PlaneMesh and an imported mesh.

summary: SkeletonModifier3D, _process_modification_with_delta(), being affected by global rotation changes

description.text:
### Tested versions

- only tested in v4.5.stable.official [876b29033]

### System information

Godot v4.5.stable - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6614) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-2500K CPU @ 3.30GHz (4 threads) - 15.98 GiB memory

### Issue description

When creating a SkeletonModifier3D and using _process_modification_with_delta(), it seems that if there is a change to the global_basis of the SkeletonModifier3D node (not just the Skeleton3D), the change takes effect halfway through the process. This causes the modification to snap back to the rest pose, even though the rotation of the SkeletonModifier3D itself should not directly affect the pose.

I understand that the modification applied by SkeletonModifier3D is discarded after each frame, which is expected behaviour. However, I’ve created an IK addon that re-applies the pose data from the end of one frame at the start of the next. This system is stable for all types of movement except when there’s rotation applied to the Skeleton3D or any of its parent nodes. In those cases, the pose momentarily resets from the rest pose back out to the IK solution’s pose.

I use slerping between poses to create smooth transitions, and that appears to be the only reason the flicker becomes visible.

This issue does not occur when only global positions change, it only happens when there’s a change in global rotation (global_basis).

### Steps to reproduce

You can clone my OSIK demo project here:
https://github.com/okaysalmon/ISOK

- In the test scene, try the following:
- Move the position of the worm in the editor it behaves as expected.
- Now try rotating the worm in the editor.

Click on the OSIK node, disable Use Slerp, and rotate it again.

You’ll notice that when Slerp is enabled, the node flickers only when the rotation changes, not when its position is moved.
If you set the Slerp Speed to around 0.1, this behaviour becomes much easier to observe. Rotating the OSIK node once set t a low seed will let you see the flicker occur in slow motion.

Disabling Slerp hides the issue because the node only returns to the rest pose for a single frame before reverting to the full IK solution. However, during that one frame, the relative solution is being overwritten.

I do think at this stage the issue is rather niche, but if more people start making more SkeletonModifier3D nodes it might become more prevalent over time.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)



https://github.com/okaysalmon/ISOK
